summary: Jeff Atwood argues that fear of breaking code is a warning sign in developers and teams. He challenges the assumption that existing code is too precious to touch, asserting that all code is inherently broken and software development is a process of continual refinement. The best way to learn a codebase is to deliberately break it in controlled ways, which builds confidence and understanding. Developers who remain afraid of breaking things will never mature into professionals.
key_points: Fear of breaking code indicates an unhealthy relationship with a codebase and stunts developer growth
All code is broken - yours, mine, everyone's - so treating it as precious is misguided
Deliberately breaking and reassembling code is the most effective way to truly understand a system
Controlled destruction transforms intimidating codebases into opportunities for improvement and even fun
Fearlessness with code changes is essential for professional development
money_quotes: The absolute worst systems I've worked on are the ones where the developers practically tiptoe around the source code.
The main problem with fear of breaking the code is the implicit assumption that any code is really that good to begin with.
All the code we write is broken. Your code. My code. Everyone's code.
Software development isn't a science; it's a process of continual refinement.
I firmly believe that the absolute best way to learn a system is to break it.
If you can't break that codebase, and then piece it back together again – in every way you can think of – then you're going to be absolutely screwed when another developer breaks something.
When it comes to software, controlled destruction breeds confidence.
Developers who are afraid of broken code are developers who will never mature into professionals.
key_insight: Controlled destruction of code builds the confidence and understanding necessary to become a professional developer, while fear of breaking things guarantees stagnation.
